contentBlocks.title: Why convert Base64 to image?
contentBlocks.html:
Base64 encodes binary image data as a text string. You encounter Base64-encoded images in CSS source code (background-image: url(data:...)), HTML <img> tags, API responses (OpenAI Vision, Google Cloud Vision), JSON/XML files, email templates, and Outlook signatures. This converter decodes them back to viewable images.

Decoding Base64 is essential for debugging frontend code, analyzing email templates, working with APIs that return images as Base64, and recovering images from configuration files or databases.

The converter auto-detects the MIME type from the data URI prefix (e.g., data:image/png;base64,) and displays a preview. It supports JPG, PNG, WebP, GIF, SVG, and BMP formats. Raw Base64 strings without prefixes are also handled — the converter attempts to detect the format automatically.

All processing happens locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server. No registration, no limits, no tracking.

contentBlocks.title: How does Base64 to image decoding work?
contentBlocks.html:
Base64 decoding is the reverse of Base64 encoding — it converts a text string back into binary image data. The converter reads the Base64 string (with or without the data URI prefix), decodes it into raw bytes, and reconstructs the original image file.

The data URI prefix (e.g., data:image/png;base64,) tells the converter which image format to use. If the prefix is missing, the converter detects the format automatically from the binary signature (magic bytes) of the decoded data.

This tool is useful when you need to extract an image from an HTML page, CSS file, API response, or database that stores images as Base64 strings. The decoded image can be saved as PNG, JPG, or any other standard format. The conversion runs entirely in your browser — no data is sent to any server.

contentBlocks.title: Tips for converting BASE64 to IMAGE
contentBlocks.description: A few tips to help you avoid common issues during conversion:

items.title: Data URI prefix
items.description: The converter accepts Base64 strings both with and without the data URI prefix (data:image/...). If the prefix is present, it is used to determine the output format.

items.title: Invalid characters
items.description: Base64 strings should only contain A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /, and = (padding). Whitespace and line breaks are automatically stripped before decoding.

items.title: Output format
items.description: The decoded image is saved in the format specified by the data URI prefix. If no prefix is provided, the converter detects the format from the image data automatically.

items.title: Large strings
items.description: Very long Base64 strings (representing large images) may take a moment to decode. The converter handles strings of any length that your browser can process.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about Base64 to image conversion

items.question: Is converting Base64 to image free?
items.answer: Yes, this converter is completely free with no limits. No registration, no watermarks.

items.question: Does the converter upload my files?
items.answer: No. All processing happens locally in your browser. Your files never leave your device.

items.question: Can I convert multiple files at once?
items.answer: Yes, the converter supports batch conversion. Add multiple files and convert them all simultaneously.

items.question: Does the converter work on mobile?
items.answer: Yes, the converter works on any device with a modern web browser, including smartphones and tablets.

items.question: Is converting Base64 to image safe?
items.answer: Yes. The entire decoding process runs locally in your browser. Your data is never sent to any server. When you close the page, all data is automatically cleared from memory.

items.question: What image formats are supported?
items.answer: The converter can decode Base64 strings into PNG, JPG, GIF, WebP, SVG, BMP, and other standard image formats. The format is detected automatically from the data URI prefix or the binary content.

items.question: Where can I find Base64 image strings?
items.answer: Base64 image strings are commonly found in HTML img src attributes, CSS background-image properties, API responses, email templates, and database records. They start with data:image/ followed by the format and base64 encoding.
